section.title: The Land Yet to Be Conquered
section.sectionRationale: The book turns from conquest to allotment. Joshua is now old, yet much land remains to be possessed, and God commands him to divide the whole land as an inheritance even before it is fully taken, promising, I Myself will drive out the nations. The chapter then records the inheritance Moses had already given the eastern tribes, and the striking note that Levi's inheritance is the LORD Himself. It is one movement, the command to apportion the land by faith, holding together God's finished promise and its ongoing fulfillment.
section.thesis: Though Joshua is old and much land is still unconquered, God tells him to divide it all by lot as an inheritance, because the giving of the land rests on God's promise, not on what has yet been achieved; and in the middle of the land grants stands a deeper truth, that for the tribe of Levi the LORD Himself is the inheritance.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the command to divide the land that remains, verses 1 to 7; the eastern inheritance Moses gave and Levi's portion in the LORD, verses 8 to 14; and the detailed allotments of Reuben, Gad, and eastern Manasseh, verses 15 to 33.

units.label: The command to divide the land that remains
units.note: I Myself will drive them out; divide it as an inheritance

verses.text: Now Joshua was old and well along in years, and the LORD said to him, “You are old and well along in years, but very much of the land remains to be possessed.
This is the land that remains: All the territory of the Philistines and the Geshurites,

from the Shihor east of Egypt to the territory of Ekron on the north (considered to be Canaanite territory), that of the five Philistine rulers of Gaza, Ashdod, Ashkelon, Gath, and Ekron, as well as that of the Avvites;
to the south, all the land of the Canaanites, from Mearah of the Sidonians to Aphek, as far as the border of the Amorites;
the land of the Gebalites; and all Lebanon to the east, from Baal-gad below Mount Hermon to Lebo-hamath.

All the inhabitants of the hill country from Lebanon to Misrephoth-maim, all the Sidonians, I Myself will {{yarash|drive out}} before the Israelites. Be sure to divide it by {{goral|lot}} as an inheritance to Israel, as I have commanded you.
Now therefore divide this land as an {{nachalah|inheritance}} to the nine tribes and the half-tribe of Manasseh.”

units.label: The eastern inheritance and Levi's portion in the LORD

verses.text: The other half of Manasseh, along with the Reubenites and Gadites, had received the inheritance Moses had given them beyond the Jordan to the east, just as Moses the servant of the LORD had assigned to them:
The area from Aroer on the rim of the Arnon Valley, along with the city in the middle of the valley, the whole plateau of Medeba as far as Dibon,
and all the cities of Sihon king of the Amorites who reigned in Heshbon, as far as the border of the Ammonites;
also Gilead and the territory of the Geshurites and Maacathites, all of Mount Hermon, and all Bashan as far as Salecah,
the whole kingdom of Og in Bashan, who had reigned in Ashtaroth and Edrei and had remained as a remnant of the Rephaim. Moses had struck them down and dispossessed them,
but the Israelites did not drive out the Geshurites or the Maacathites. So Geshur and Maacath dwell among the Israelites to this day.
To the tribe of Levi, however, Moses had given no inheritance. The food offerings to the LORD, the God of Israel, are their inheritance, just as He had promised them.

units.label: The allotments of Reuben, Gad, and eastern Manasseh
units.note: the portions Moses gave on the plains of Moab

verses.text: This is what Moses had given to the clans of the tribe of Reuben:
The territory from Aroer on the rim of the Arnon Valley, along with the city in the middle of the valley, to the whole plateau beyond Medeba,
to Heshbon and all its cities on the plateau, including Dibon, Bamoth-baal, Beth-baal-meon,
Jahaz, Kedemoth, Mephaath,
Kiriathaim, Sibmah, Zereth-shahar on the hill in the valley,
Beth-peor, the slopes of Pisgah, and Beth-jeshimoth,

all the cities of the plateau and all the kingdom of Sihon king of the Amorites, who reigned in Heshbon until Moses killed him and the chiefs of Midian (Evi, Rekem, Zur, Hur, and Reba), the princes of Sihon who lived in the land.
The Israelites also killed the diviner Balaam son of Beor along with the others they put to the sword.
And the border of the Reubenites was the bank of the Jordan. This was the inheritance of the clans of the Reubenites, including the cities and villages.
This is what Moses had given to the clans of the tribe of Gad:
The territory of Jazer, all the cities of Gilead, and half the land of the Ammonites as far as Aroer, near Rabbah;
the territory from Heshbon to Ramath-mizpeh and Betonim, and from Mahanaim to the border of Debir;

and in the valley, Beth-haram, Beth-nimrah, Succoth, and Zaphon, with the rest of the kingdom of Sihon king of Heshbon (the territory on the east side of the Jordan up to the edge of the Sea of Chinnereth).
This was the inheritance of the clans of the Gadites, including the cities and villages.
This is what Moses had given to the clans of the half-tribe of Manasseh, that is, to half the tribe of the descendants of Manasseh:
The territory from Mahanaim through all Bashan, all the kingdom of Og king of Bashan, including all the towns of Jair that are in Bashan, sixty cities;
half of Gilead; and Ashtaroth and Edrei, the royal cities of Og in Bashan. All this was for the clans of the descendants of Machir son of Manasseh, that is, half of the descendants of Machir.
These were the portions Moses had given them on the plains of Moab beyond the Jordan, east of Jericho.
To the tribe of Levi, however, Moses had given no inheritance. The LORD, the God of Israel, is their inheritance, just as He had promised them.

yarash.gloss: To take possession of, dispossess, drive out. God promises, I Myself will drive out the remaining nations, using the key conquest verb but now with God as the emphatic subject. The land still to be possessed will be taken not by Israel's exhausted, aging strength but by the LORD Himself, so the tribes may divide it in faith while the driving out continues.

goral.gloss: A lot, the casting of lots to determine God's decision. The land is divided by goral, so that each tribe's portion is seen as assigned by the LORD, not won by favoritism or human bargaining, Proverbs 16:33. The lot removes human striving from the allotment and grounds each inheritance in God's own choice.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, allotted possession, heritage. The land is Israel's nachalah, divided among the tribes as a gift by promise. Yet the chapter's deepest note is that Levi's nachalah is not land at all: the LORD Himself, and the offerings made to Him, are their inheritance, a truth that points to the highest good of all God's people, to have God as their portion.

basic.context: The conquest is largely done, and Joshua is now an old man, but God points out that much land still remains to be taken, and He commands Joshua to divide the whole land among the tribes as an inheritance, even the parts not yet possessed. The reason is a promise: I Myself will drive them out before you. The tribes are to receive their portions by faith, trusting God to complete what He has begun. The chapter then records the land Moses had already given the eastern tribes, Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh, in detail. Twice it pauses to say something remarkable about the tribe of Levi: they get no land, because the LORD Himself is their inheritance. The question underneath is: how do God's people live between a promise given and a promise not yet fully realized, and what is the greatest inheritance of all?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that shifts the book from conquest to allotment. The detailed geography is not directly applicable to us, but the chapter carries rich theology: God gives the inheritance as a promise to be received by faith even before it is fully seen, and the LORD Himself is the true inheritance of His people. The note that Israel did not drive out some peoples also honestly flags the incomplete obedience that will trouble later chapters.
basic.originalLanguages: God says He will Himself yarash, drive out, the nations that remain. The land is divided by goral, by lot, as God's own assignment. It is Israel's nachalah, inheritance; yet Levi's nachalah is the LORD Himself.
basic.typology: The already and not yet of the land, given by promise but not fully possessed, pictures the Christian's inheritance in Christ, secured and yet awaited. And Levi having the LORD as their inheritance foreshadows the deepest truth of the gospel, that God Himself is the portion of His people, fulfilled in Christ.
basic.christ: Levi's portion, the LORD Himself, points to the greatest gift of the gospel: not merely things from God but God Himself, given to us in Christ. As the psalmist says, the LORD is my portion; so in Christ we receive God as our inheritance and are made His. The land held by promise yet still being possessed mirrors our inheritance, guaranteed in Christ and awaiting full possession.
basic.god: God is the faithful promise-keeper who commands His people to act on His word before it is fully fulfilled, pledging, I Myself will drive them out. He is the God who assigns each portion by lot, sovereign over the inheritance. And He is Himself the highest inheritance, the portion of His people, greater than any land.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 18 explains that the LORD is the Levites' portion. Psalm 16 rejoices that the LORD is my chosen portion and inheritance. Deuteronomy 3 records the eastern allotment under Moses. And Ephesians 1 speaks of the inheritance guaranteed to believers in Christ by the Spirit.
basic.oneStory: As the land is divided, God commands His people to receive the inheritance by faith while He completes the driving out, and He reveals that for Levi the LORD Himself is the inheritance. This points to the gospel: an inheritance secured in Christ yet awaited, and the greatest gift of all, God Himself as the portion of His people.

context.literary: Chapter 13 opens the second half of Joshua, the allotment of the land, chapters 13 to 21, balancing the conquest of chapters 1 to 12. It begins with a fresh word from the LORD, marking a new section, and repeatedly uses the language of inheritance. The detailed boundary and city lists, which continue through the following chapters, give the allotments the character of legal land grants. Two nearly identical statements about Levi, verses 14 and 33, frame the eastern allotments and highlight the theological center: the LORD as Levi's portion.
context.historical: Joshua's age signals that the era of active conquest is closing and the settlement is beginning; the remaining lands, Philistine territory on the coast and Phoenician areas to the north, would indeed remain largely unconquered for generations. Allotment by lot, overseen by the priest and leaders, was the accepted, God-honoring way to distribute land fairly among tribes. The eastern tribes had already received their inheritance from Moses after the defeat of Sihon and Og, Numbers 32 and Deuteronomy 3. The note about Balaam recalls his failed attempt to curse Israel, Numbers 22 to 24, and his death in the Midianite war, Numbers 31.
context.canonical: The chapter develops the biblical theme of inheritance, which runs from the land promise to Abraham to the New Testament's imperishable inheritance in Christ. The Levites' portion, the LORD Himself, becomes a treasured thread in Scripture, echoed in the Psalms, the LORD is my portion, and fulfilled when God gives Himself to His people in Christ and by the Spirit. The honest note of incomplete conquest, some peoples not driven out, sets up the recurring tension in Joshua and Judges between God's complete gift and Israel's incomplete obedience.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record God's command to divide the whole land as an inheritance while much remained unconquered, resting the allotment on God's promise to drive out the nations Himself, to document the eastern tribes' inheritance from Moses, and to highlight that the LORD Himself is the inheritance of the tribe of Levi.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific boundaries and allotments describe Israel's historical settlement and are not a template for land today. What endures is the theology: God's people receive His promises by faith even before full possession, God sovereignly assigns each portion, incomplete obedience has real consequences, and the highest inheritance of God's people is God Himself.

debates.question: Why command the land divided when so much was still unconquered?

views.label: Because the inheritance rests on God's promise, not on completed conquest
views.case: God says I Myself will drive them out; the tribes are to receive their portions in faith, trusting God to finish what He began, so the allotment is an act of confidence in the promise, teaching that God's people lay hold of His gifts by faith before they see them fully realized, Hebrews 11:1.

views.label: To set a mandate that would summon ongoing faith and obedience
views.case: By assigning land not yet taken, God gives each tribe both a gift and a task, a portion to possess as they continue to trust and obey; the arrangement guards against complacency, since the later failure to drive out the nations, already hinted in verse 13, will show what happens when faith and obedience falter.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold the already and not yet together: the inheritance is truly given by promise, yet it must be entered by faith, a pattern that shapes the Christian life lived between Christ's finished work and its full consummation.

study.originalLanguages: Three words open the door to this chapter's theology. God promises that He Himself will yarash, drive out, the nations that still hold the land; the emphatic I Myself places the completion of the conquest on God's shoulders, not on Joshua's aging strength, so the tribes may divide the land in faith while the driving out goes on. The land is then apportioned by goral, by lot, so that each portion is received as God's own assignment rather than the fruit of favoritism or human bargaining, for the lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD, Proverbs 16:33. And the land is Israel's nachalah, its inheritance by promise, a heritage given rather than earned. Yet the chapter's most striking use of nachalah is the exception: to Levi God gave no land, because the LORD, the God of Israel, is their inheritance, verses 14 and 33. That is the deepest note of all, sounded twice to be sure we hear it: the highest possession is not a portion of ground but God Himself, the truth that becomes the joy of every believer, the LORD is my chosen portion, Psalm 16:5.

typology.type: The land given by promise yet not fully possessed
typology.fulfillment: The believer's inheritance in Christ, secured and guaranteed yet awaiting full possession, Ephesians 1:13-14 and Hebrews 11:13-16.

typology.type: The LORD Himself as the inheritance of Levi
typology.fulfillment: God given as the portion of His people in Christ, so that we possess not merely gifts from God but God Himself, Psalm 16:5 and Romans 8:17.

typology.type: God's pledge, I Myself will drive them out
typology.fulfillment: God completing the salvation He begins in His people, working in them to finish what He started, Philippians 1:6 and 1 Thessalonians 5:24.

study.christ: The quiet center of this chapter is one of the most gospel-rich lines in Joshua: the tribe of Levi received no land, for the LORD Himself is their inheritance. That is the very heart of what Christ came to give. The other tribes received portions of ground; Levi received God. And in Christ, this becomes the inheritance of all God's people: not merely blessings and gifts from God, but God Himself, our portion and our exceeding joy. The psalmist sings, the LORD is my chosen portion and my cup, Psalm 16:5, and Paul says that in Christ we are heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ, Romans 8:17. The land held by promise but not yet fully possessed also pictures the Christian life: our inheritance is secured and sealed by the Spirit as a guarantee, yet we await its full possession, Ephesians 1:13-14. And the God who says I Myself will drive them out is the God who promises to complete in us the good work He has begun, Philippians 1:6. Christ is both the guarantee of the inheritance and, wonderfully, the inheritance itself, God with us and God for us.
study.god: This chapter shows God as promise-keeper, sovereign apportioner, and supreme inheritance. He is faithful to His word, so confident in His own promise that He has the land divided before it is fully taken, pledging, I Myself will drive them out; the completion depends on Him, not on His people's strength. He is sovereign over the inheritance, assigning each portion by lot so that no tribe can claim to have won its place by favor or force. And most gloriously, He gives Himself: for the Levites there is no land, because the LORD is their inheritance, a statement that reveals the highest good God offers, not things from His hand but His own presence and fellowship. The God of Joshua 13 is a God who keeps His promises, distributes His gifts justly, and, best of all, gives His people Himself as their everlasting portion.

crossReferences.note: The LORD tells Aaron, I am your portion and your inheritance among the Israelites, the truth restated here about Levi.
The LORD is my chosen portion and my cup; the lines have fallen for me in pleasant places, the believer's echo of Levi's inheritance.
Moses records giving the eastern land to Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh, the allotments detailed here.
The Holy Spirit is the guarantee of our inheritance in Christ until we take full possession of it.
He who began a good work in you will complete it, echoing God's pledge to drive out the nations Himself.

study.oneStory: As the land is divided, God commands His people to receive their inheritance by faith while He Himself completes the driving out, holding together the promise given and the promise not yet fully realized. And in the middle of the land grants stands the deepest truth of all: for Levi, the LORD Himself is the inheritance. This points straight to the gospel, an inheritance secured in Christ yet awaited in full, and the greatest gift imaginable, God Himself given to be the portion of His people forever.

quiz.q: What did God tell the aging Joshua about the land?

options.text: That much land still remained to be possessed, and it should be divided as an inheritance even so.
options.note: Verses 1 to 7 record God's command to divide the land, though much was unconquered.

options.text: That the conquest was entirely finished and nothing remained.
options.note: The opposite: God stressed that very much of the land remained to be possessed.

options.text: That Israel had failed and would lose the land.
options.note: God was not pronouncing failure; He promised to drive out the nations and commanded the land divided.

options.text: That Joshua must conquer it all himself before dividing it.
options.note: God said He Himself would drive out the nations and told Joshua to divide the land now, by faith.

quiz.q: How was the land to be divided among the tribes?

options.text: By lot, so that each portion was received as the LORD's own assignment.
options.note: Verse 6 commands division by lot, grounding each inheritance in God's choice.

options.text: By auction to the highest bidder.
options.note: There was no auction; the land was assigned by lot as God's gift, not sold.

options.text: By letting each tribe seize whatever it could take.
options.note: The allotment was orderly and by lot, not a free-for-all seizure.

options.text: By Joshua's personal preference for each tribe.
options.note: It was decided by lot as God's assignment, not by Joshua's favoritism.

quiz.q: What was the inheritance of the tribe of Levi?

options.text: The LORD Himself, and the offerings made to Him; they received no land.
options.note: Verses 14 and 33 state twice that the LORD is Levi's inheritance.

options.text: The largest and best portion of land.
options.note: Levi received no land portion at all; their inheritance was the LORD Himself.

options.text: A share of every other tribe's harvest, but a small plot of land too.
options.note: They lived among cities with pasturelands, but the text stresses they had no land inheritance; the LORD was their portion.

options.text: Nothing at all, not even God's provision.
options.note: They had the greatest inheritance of all, the LORD Himself, and His offerings for provision.

quiz.q: Why did God command the land divided before it was fully conquered?

options.text: Because the inheritance rests on God's promise to drive out the nations, received by faith.
options.note: God's pledge, I Myself will drive them out, grounds the allotment in faith, not in completed conquest.

options.text: Because God had given up on conquering the rest.
options.note: God had not given up; He promised to complete the driving out Himself.

options.text: Because the tribes demanded their land immediately.
options.note: The command came from God, resting on His promise, not from the tribes' demands.

options.text: Because the remaining land did not matter.
options.note: The land mattered and was included in the inheritance; God promised to secure it.

quiz.q: How does Levi's inheritance point to the gospel?

options.text: Having the LORD Himself as their portion pictures the gift of God Himself to His people in Christ.
options.note: The LORD as Levi's inheritance foreshadows God given as our portion in Christ, Psalm 16:5, Romans 8:17.

options.text: It shows that God's people should expect no blessings at all.
options.note: Levi lacked land, but had the greatest blessing, God Himself; the point is not the absence of blessing.

options.text: It teaches that only priests can truly know God.
options.note: In Christ all believers have God as their portion, not only a priestly class.

options.text: It has no connection to the gospel.
options.note: The LORD as inheritance is one of the richest gospel pointers, fulfilled in God giving Himself to us in Christ.

questions: God told the tribes to receive land by faith before it was fully theirs. Where is God calling you to lay hold of one of His promises by faith before you can see it fully realized?
The land was assigned by lot, as God's own choosing. How does trusting that your circumstances are assigned by a sovereign, loving God change the way you view your lot in life?
Levi's inheritance was the LORD Himself. Is God Himself your treasure, or are you mainly seeking the gifts He gives rather than God?
Some peoples were not driven out, a hint of incomplete obedience that would cause later trouble. Where might partial obedience in your life be leaving trouble to grow?
In Christ, God gives His people not just blessings but Himself. How would your prayer and worship change if you truly prized having God as your portion above all else?

translationNotes.note: The BSB sets off the clause about Canaanite territory with an em dash; this has been rendered as a comma here, with no change to the wording.

The BSB sets off all the Sidonians with em dashes; these have been rendered as commas here. The same is done for the trailing em dashes in verses 11 and 20, which connect the boundary lists across verses.
Balaam son of Beor, the diviner who tried to curse Israel in Numbers 22 to 24, was killed in the war against Midian, Numbers 31:8; the note underscores the failure of his hire to curse God's people.

The food offerings to the LORD are the Levites' inheritance in verse 14, while verse 33 says the LORD Himself is their inheritance; the two express one truth, that God and His service are Levi's portion in place of land.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 13:1-33. Prose with boundary and city lists; line-break markers within verses were ignored and segments joined with single spaces. The em dashes in verses 3, 6, 11, and 20 were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
